perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees that Buyer and its Representatives shall (i) use the Ev aluation Material solely for the purpose of evaluating, negotiating and consummating th e Transaction (or other legal, audit or compliance purposes permitted by this Agreement);13 (ii) except as otherwise permitted by this Section 2, keep all Evaluation Material strictly confidential; and (iii) disclose Evaluation Material only to Representatives of Buyer to whom disclosure is needed to facilitate the evaluation, negotiation and/or consummation of the Transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees that Buyer and its Representatives shall (i) use the Evaluation Material solely for the purpose of evaluating, negotiating and consummating the Transaction; (ii) except as otherwise permitted by this Section 2, keep all Evaluation Material strictly confidential.
perturbation.explanation: By removing clause (iii), which restricts disclosure of Evaluation Material to only those representatives that need it, the contract is weakened. This could be viewed as a contradiction of New York's data security laws, which emphasize limited access to sensitive information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York SHIELD Act - General Business Law Section 899-bb
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's SHIELD Act requires businesses to implement reasonable safeguards to protect the private information of New York residents. Omitting the restriction on disclosing Evaluation Material only to necessary representatives weakens data security measures, potentially violating the requirement to implement reasonable safeguards. The SHIELD Act calls for reasonable security, and this would be an instance of unreasonable disclosure.

perturbation.justification: The original contract includes a clause (iii) that restricts the disclosure of Evaluation Material to only representatives who need it. By removing this clause, the changed contract broadens the scope of potential recipients, weakening data security. The Law Explanation states that New York's SHIELD Act requires reasonable safeguards to protect private information, and the Perturbation Explanation specifies that omitting the restriction on disclosing Evaluation Material potentially violates this requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses NYS General Business Law Article 39-F, which pertains to data security protections. While the snippet is incomplete, it suggests the existence of data protection regulations in New York, supporting the claim that the omission contradicts state law related to data security and reasonable safeguards.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees that all communications by it or any of its Representatives with the ot her Party concerning the Transaction and its due diligence investigation (including requests for additional Evaluation Material, meetings with management and site visits) shall be directed solely to [___________________] , except as may otherwise be approved in advance and in writing (including by email) by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees that all communications by it or any of its Representatives with the other Party concerning the Transaction and its due diligence investigation shall be directed to individuals the Buyer deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the requirement for communications to be directed solely to a designated point of contact, the contract becomes less restrictive. This is a contradiction to New York's Personal Privacy Protection Law because it increases risk of accidental disclosure to unauthorized parties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Personal Privacy Protection Law - Public Officers Law, Article 6-A
perturbation.law_explanation: New York's Personal Privacy Protection Law requires agencies to safeguard personal information and prevent unauthorized access. Removing the designated point of contact increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure, violating the requirement to safeguard personal information. This is because the original clause creates an accountability checkpoint while this removed clause would allow for free communications.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Personal Privacy Protection Law | Open Government Skip to main content Committee on Open Government Personal Privacy Protection Law Pertains to personal information collected and maintained by state agencies. TOP Personal Privacy Protection Law SHARE Share by Email Share by Facebook Share by Twitter See All Overview The Personal Privacy Protection Law (PPPL), enacted in 1984, pertains to personal information collected and maintained by state agencies. State agencies are required to meet standards of fair information practices regarding the collection, maintenance, use and disclosure of personal information. PPPL Law Text Personal Privacy Protection Law (PPPL) Text PUBLIC OFFICERS LAW, ARTICLE 6-A PERSONAL PRIVACY PROTECTION LAW Download Advisory Opinions The opinions represent the views of the Committee on Open Government at the time that they were rendered. They may no longer represent those views if, for example, there have been subsequent court decisions or statutory amendments that bear on the issues discussed in the opinions. Due to the repetition of the substance of various opinions, not all opinions are available online. Only those that have been determined to have precedential value or that are in some manner unique are included. Advisory opinions that are not available online, primarily those prepared prior to 1993, are maintained by the Committee on Open Government and at various law libraries throughout the state. Copies of opinions are available by contacting the Committee . search opinions Case Law Summary see foil cases Other Publications “ You Should Know ” is a publication of the Committee on Open Government that provides an overview of the Personal Privacy Protection Law (PPPL). The PPPL is intended to protect your privacy by regulating the manner in which state agencies collect, maintain and disseminate personal information about you. The publication reviews state agency responsibilities, as well as guidance on how to obtain state agency records about yourself and, if necessary, how to correct or amend your record. The publication includes sample language of a PPPL request and, if necessary, on appeal of an agency’s denial of access. 2020 Annual Report Download Frequently Asked Questions Personal Privacy Protection Law Frequently Asked Questions Download Open Government
perturbation.justification: The original contract text mandates that all communications be directed to a specific point of contact, ensuring accountability and control over information flow. The changed text removes this requirement, allowing communications to be directed to anyone the Buyer deems appropriate. This omission contradicts the principle of safeguarding personal information outlined in New York's Personal Privacy Protection Law (PPPL), as explained by the LLM. Although the first snippet is unavailable, the second snippet states the PPPL "pertains to personal information collected and maintained by state agencies" and requires agencies to "meet standards of fair information practices regarding the collection, maintenance, use and disclosure of personal information." By removing the designated point of contact, the risk of unauthorized disclosure of personal information increases, directly contravening the PPPL's intent to protect privacy through regulated information handling practices.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for a breach or a threatened breach of this Agreement and that each party shall be entitled to specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief, without the posting of a bond or other security, as a remedy for any such breach or threatened breach, in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for a breach or a threatened breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the entitlement to specific performance and injunctive relief, the contract limits the remedies available. Under New York law, parties are usually entitled to equitable relief in cases involving confidential information, so limiting this could be seen as a contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 6301
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 6301 outlines the grounds for preliminary injunctions. By removing the explicit entitlement to injunctive relief, the agreement may be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the court's power to grant such relief, particularly in cases where monetary damages are inadequate, such as breaches of confidentiality, potentially conflicting with established legal principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit entitlement to specific performance and injunctive relief, which the original text provided. The perturbation explanation and Law Explanation both suggest this removal could conflict with established legal principles, especially in cases where monetary damages are inadequate. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unavailable and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is nonexistent, the core idea is that removing the entitlement weakens a party's ability to seek equitable relief, potentially contradicting the court's power to grant such relief. This aligns with the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction' where a legally relevant protection is omitted, leading to potential non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer acknowledges and agrees that Representatives of Buyer who have received or have been exposed to Evaluation Material may further develop their general knowledge, skills and experience (including general ideas, concepts, know- how and techniques), which may be based in whole or in part on such Evaluation Material. Notwithstanding anything in this Agreement to the contrary, the subsequent use by such Representative of such general knowledge, skills and experience, as retained in their unaided memories, will not in itself constitute a breach of this Agreement. 27
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer acknowledges and agrees that Representatives of Buyer who have received or have been exposed to Evaluation Material may further develop their general knowledge, skills and experience, which may be based in whole or in part on such Evaluation Material.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'residual knowledge' clause's protection for unaided memory, it implies all derived knowledge is restricted, which can contradict New York's trade secret law, which requires specificity and demonstrable competitive harm. It also contradicts established principles of knowledge acquisition and career development. Employees have rights to their developed knowledge.
perturbation.law_citation: Ashland Management Inc. v. Janien, 82 N.Y.2d 395, 604 N.Y.S.2d 912, 624 N.E.2d 1007 (1993)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York trade secret law requires a showing of competitive harm and specificity in defining trade secrets. By removing the protection for unaided memory, the NDA effectively tries to restrict the use of general knowledge and skills, which is far broader than protected trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the principles established in cases like Ashland Management Inc. v. Janien. The absence of this clause could give the company grounds to wrongly sue an employee.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Ashland Mgt. v. Janien :: 1993 :: New York Court of Appeals Decisions :: New York Case Law :: New York Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Ashland Mgt. v. Janien Annotate this Case 82 N.Y.2d 395 (1993) 624 N.E.2d 1007 604 N.Y.S.2d 912 Ashland Management Incorporated, Appellant, v. C. Christopher Janien, Respondent. (And a Third-Party Action.) Court of Appeals of the State of New York. Argued October 14, 1993. Decided November 22, 1993. Paul, Hastings, Janofsky & Walker, New York City (Lawrence I. Weinstein, Lisa M. Buckley and Roger M. Milgrim of counsel), for appellant. Davis & Gilbert, New York City (Michael C. Lasky, Bruce M. Ginsberg and Howard Weingrad of counsel), for respondent. Chief Judge KAYE and Judges TITONE, HANCOCK, JR., BELLACOSA, SMITH and LEVINE concur. *399SIMONS, J. This litigation involves a dispute between an investment management firm and a former employee. The employee developed a mathematical model for determining investment strategy and sought unsuccessfully to sell it to the firm. A dispute arose during the negotiations and plaintiff, Ashland Management Inc., instituted this action seeking to foreclose defendant from using his model. It alleged that the model is based upon trade secrets defendant misappropriated from the firm. Defendant claims he and plaintiff had agreed upon a contract for the use of the model and that plaintiff breached the contract. Defendant, therefore, counterclaimed seeking damages. After a nonjury trial, Supreme Court found that the parties had entered into a contract and that plaintiff had breached it. Accordingly, the court awarded defendant damages of $625,000 for lost profits. It held further that the information available to defendant did not constitute a trade secret of Ashland and therefore defendant was not guilty of misappropriation. The Appellate Division, after a minor modification, affirmed the judgment. The principal legal questions presented concern the availability of damages for lost profits under the rule stated in our two decisions in Kenford Co. v County of Erie (67 N.Y.2d 257 [Kenford I]; and 73 N.Y.2d 312 [Kenford II]) and whether the court applied the correct legal standard for trade secrets in determining t
perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'residual knowledge' clause in the changed text creates a legal contradiction because it broadens the restrictions on the use of knowledge, potentially conflicting with New York trade secret law. The original text protected the use of general knowledge, skills, and experience retained in unaided memories. By removing this protection, the NDA now arguably attempts to restrict general knowledge and skills. The Law Explanation explicitly states that the absence of this clause could give the company grounds to wrongly sue an employee. Scraped Law Snippet 2 cites Ashland Mgt. v. Janien, indicating that New York courts have considered the issue of trade secrets in relation to former employees. The changed text could be interpreted as an attempt to prevent a former employee from using knowledge gained during their employment, which is broader than the definition of a trade secret, potentially leading to a legal challenge based on the principles established in Ashland Mgt. v. Janien.
